abstract: Despite rapid advances in autonomous AI scientists powered by language models, generating publication-ready illustrations remains a labor-intensive bottleneck in the research workflow. To lift this burden, we introduce PaperBanana, an agentic framework for automated generation of publication-ready academic illustrations. Powered by state-of-the-art VLMs and image generation models, PaperBanana orchestrates specialized agents to retrieve references, plan content and style, render images, and iteratively refine via self-critique. To rigorously evaluate our framework, we introduce PaperBananaBench, comprising 292 test cases for methodology diagrams curated from NeurIPS 2025 publications, covering diverse research domains and illustration styles. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that PaperBanana consistently outperforms leading baselines in faithfulness, conciseness, readability, and aesthetics. We further show that our method effectively extends to the generation of high-quality statistical plots. Collectively, PaperBanana paves the way for the automated generation of publication-ready illustrations.

keyInsights: An agentic framework with iterative refinement, guided by aesthetic guidelines and critical feedback, effectively improves the quality and academic adherence of generated illustrations.
Reference-based retrieval provides essential structural logic and stylistic guidance, significantly enhancing the generation process for both methodology diagrams and statistical plots.

While image generation models offer broad presentation capabilities, code-based visualization is crucial for ensuring numerical precision and data fidelity in statistical plots, highlighting a trade-off.
originalProblem: The generation of high-quality, publication-ready academic illustrations (methodology diagrams, statistical plots) is a labor-intensive bottleneck in scientific communication.
Existing code-based diagram generation methods lack the expressiveness for complex visual elements and specialized icons common in modern AI publications.
General image generation models struggle to consistently produce academic illustrations that meet rigorous content fidelity and aesthetic standards required for scholarly publishing.
results: PaperBanana improved the aggregated overall quality score by 17.0% for methodology diagrams on PaperBananaBench compared to vanilla baselines, confirmed by a 72.7% win/tie rate in human evaluations.
The framework achieved an overall quality improvement of 4.1% for statistical plots, with performance gains across faithfulness, conciseness, readability, and aesthetics.

Ablation studies demonstrated that the Retriever, Stylist, and Critic agents are crucial for enhancing various quality dimensions, with the Critic agent specifically balancing aesthetic improvements with content faithfulness during iterative refinement.
solution: PaperBanana introduces a reference-driven agentic framework comprising five specialized agents: Retriever, Planner, Stylist, Visualizer, and Critic, to automate illustration generation.

It leverages visual language models (VLMs) like Gemini-3-Pro for tasks such as generative retrieval, planning textual descriptions, synthesizing aesthetic guidelines, and providing iterative criticism.
A new comprehensive benchmark, PaperBananaBench, was constructed for methodology diagram generation, using a VLM-as-a-Judge approach for reference-based evaluation across four criteria.
paper_summary.summary: An agentic AI system, PaperBanana, automates the generation of publication-ready scientific illustrations, including methodology diagrams and statistical plots, from research paper text. The system achieved a 17.0% improvement in overall quality for methodology diagrams on the new PaperBananaBench and a 4.1% overall improvement for statistical plots by leveraging visual language models for iterative refinement.

title: PaperBanana: Automating Academic Illustration for AI Scientists

abstract:
Historically, scientific discovery has been a lengthy and costly process,
demanding substantial time and resources from initial conception to final
results. To accelerate scientific discovery, reduce research costs, and improve
research quality, we introduce Agent Laboratory, an autonomous LLM-based
framework capable of completing the entire research process. This framework
accepts a human-provided research idea and progresses through three
stages--literature review, experimentation, and report writing to produce
comprehensive research outputs, including a code repository and a research
report, while enabling users to provide feedback and guidance at each stage. We
deploy Agent Laboratory with various state-of-the-art LLMs and invite multiple
researchers to assess its quality by participating in a survey, providing human
feedback to guide the research process, and then evaluate the final paper. We
found that: (1) Agent Laboratory driven by o1-preview generates the best
research outcomes; (2) The generated machine learning code is able to achieve
state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods; (3) Human
involvement, providing feedback at each stage, significantly improves the
overall quality of research; (4) Agent Laboratory significantly reduces
research expenses, achieving an 84% decrease compared to previous autonomous
research methods. We hope Agent Laboratory enables researchers to allocate more
effort toward creative ideation rather than low-level coding and writing,
ultimately accelerating scientific discovery.

keyInsights: Human-AI collaboration, particularly in a "co-pilot" model, is effective for improving the quality of AI-generated research outputs.
Automated evaluation of research quality often overestimates results compared to human assessment, underscoring the need for human validation.
The choice of LLM backend significantly impacts research outcomes, with `o1-preview` and `o1-mini` performing better for specific tasks than `gpt-4o`.
originalProblem: Scientific research is a lengthy, resource-intensive process that limits the number of ideas researchers can explore simultaneously.
Researchers are often burdened by repetitive, low-level tasks, hindering their focus on creative ideation and high-level experiment design.
The high cost and time investment in conducting research create a bottleneck in accelerating scientific discovery.
results: Reduced research costs by 84%, with a full paper generation costing as little as $2.33 using `gpt-4o`, which also completed the workflow in approximately 19.4 minutes.
The `mle-solver` component achieved state-of-the-art performance on MLE-Bench Kaggle challenges, earning more medals than competitors and surpassing median human performance on 60% of benchmarks.
Human intervention in "co-pilot mode" notably improved the overall quality of research outputs, with papers rated higher (4.38/10) compared to autonomously generated papers (3.8/10).
solution: An LLM-based multi-agent framework orchestrates the entire research process through distinct sequential phases: Literature Review, Experimentation, and Report Writing.
Specialized agents (e.g., PhD, ML Engineer) collaborate within the pipeline, utilizing external tools and iterative refinement loops powered by modules like `mle-solver` and `paper-solver`.
The framework operates in both autonomous mode and a human-guided "co-pilot" mode, allowing researchers to intervene at checkpoints and provide feedback to refine agent performance.
paper_summary.summary: Agent Laboratory, an LLM-based framework from AMD and academic partners, automates the scientific research process, notably reducing research costs to $2.33 per paper and enabling state-of-the-art performance in automated machine learning problem-solving. Human involvement in its co-pilot mode improves the overall quality of research outputs.

title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLM Agents as Research Assistants

abstract: One of the grand challenges of artificial general intelligence is developing agents capable of conducting scientific research and discovering new knowledge. While frontier models have already been used as aides to human scientists, e.g. for brainstorming ideas, writing code, or prediction tasks, they still conduct only a small part of the scientific process. This paper presents the first comprehensive framework for fully automatic scientific discovery, enabling frontier large language models to perform research independently and communicate their findings. We introduce The AI Scientist, which generates novel research ideas, writes code, executes experiments, visualizes results, describes its findings by writing a full scientific paper, and then runs a simulated review process for evaluation. In principle, this process can be repeated to iteratively develop ideas in an open-ended fashion, acting like the human scientific community. We demonstrate its versatility by applying it to three distinct subfields of machine learning: diffusion modeling, transformer-based language modeling, and learning dynamics. Each idea is implemented and developed into a full paper at a cost of less than $15 per paper. To evaluate the generated papers, we design and validate an automated reviewer, which we show achieves near-human performance in evaluating paper scores. The AI Scientist can produce papers that exceed the acceptance threshold at a top machine learning conference as judged by our automated reviewer. This approach signifies the beginning of a new era in scientific discovery in machine learning: bringing the transformative benefits of AI agents to the entire research process of AI itself, and taking us closer to a world where endless affordable creativity and innovation can be unleashed on the world's most challenging problems. Our code is open-sourced at this https URL

keyInsights: AI agents can autonomously perform the entire scientific research lifecycle, from ideation and experimentation to paper writing and simulated peer review.
Integrating LLMs with external tools (like Aider for coding and Semantic Scholar for literature search) enables robust, iterative experimentation and knowledge synthesis.

Automated LLM-based peer reviewers can achieve near-human-level accuracy in evaluating scientific papers, offering a scalable solution for quality control and feedback in an autonomous research ecosystem.
originalProblem: Traditional human-driven scientific discovery is slow, labor-intensive, and limited by human ingenuity and time, hindering the pace of knowledge generation.
Existing AI applications in science primarily assist human researchers or automate constrained tasks, lacking an end-to-end framework for fully autonomous research.
There is a need to democratize scientific research by lowering financial and intellectual barriers, accelerating progress across various domains.
results: The AI Scientist generated hundreds of medium-quality papers at an approximate cost of $10-$15 per paper, leveraging LLM API costs for coding and writing.
A GPT-4o-based automated reviewer achieved near-human-level balanced accuracy (0.65) and a superhuman F1 Score (0.57) compared to human reviewers on ICLR 2022 papers, costing $0.25-$0.50 per review.

The system successfully implemented complex algorithmic changes and generated novel visualizations in case studies (e.g., 'Adaptive Dual-Scale Denoising'), though it exhibited issues like subtle code errors, hallucinations, and a positive interpretation bias.
solution: An end-to-end framework called "The AI Scientist" was developed, leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform iterative cycles of idea generation, experimental iteration, and scientific paper write-up.
The system integrates tools like Aider for autonomous code generation and execution, and connects to the Semantic Scholar API for novelty checks, related work, and citation management.
An LLM-based automated reviewer mimics conference guidelines to evaluate generated papers, providing numerical scores and textual feedback that can loop back into the discovery process.
paper_summary.summary: Sakana AI and collaborating institutions developed a comprehensive framework for fully automated scientific discovery, enabling AI agents to conduct an entire research endeavor from hypothesis to peer-reviewed paper. This system generated hundreds of medium-quality papers in machine learning at a cost of under $15 per paper, with its automated reviewer achieving near-human-level performance.

title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery

abstract: Recent advances in deep-research systems have demonstrated the potential for AI agents to autonomously discover and synthesize knowledge from external sources. In this paper, we introduce WebResearcher, a novel framework for building such agents through two key components: (1) WebResearcher, an iterative deep-research paradigm that reformulates deep research as a Markov Decision Process, where agents periodically consolidate findings into evolving reports while maintaining focused workspaces, overcoming the context suffocation and noise contamination that plague existing mono-contextual approaches; and (2) WebFrontier, a scalable data synthesis engine that generates high-quality training data through tool-augmented complexity escalation, enabling systematic creation of research tasks that bridge the gap between passive knowledge recall and active knowledge construction. Notably, we find that the training data from our paradigm significantly enhances tool-use capabilities even for traditional mono-contextual methods. Furthermore, our paradigm naturally scales through parallel thinking, enabling concurrent multi-agent exploration for more comprehensive conclusions. Extensive experiments across 6 challenging benchmarks demonstrate that WebResearcher achieves state-of-the-art performance, even surpassing frontier proprietary systems.

keyInsights: Redefining deep research as a Markov Decision Process with iterative synthesis and workspace reconstruction effectively mitigates context degradation and error propagation.
High-quality training data, generated by systematically escalating cognitive complexity with tool use, is essential and can significantly enhance agent capabilities.
Leveraging a multi-agent Research-Synthesis Framework at inference time improves robustness and accuracy by integrating diverse reasoning paths.
originalProblem: Existing deep-research AI agents suffer from "cognitive workspace suffocation" due to continuously expanding context windows in their mono-contextual paradigm.
Irreversible noise contamination and accumulation of irrelevant information degrade reasoning quality over long research horizons.
There is a critical scarcity of high-quality, complex training data for developing robust tool-augmented deep-research agents.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across six challenging benchmarks, outperforming larger general LLMs and existing proprietary/open-source deep-research systems.
Secured 36.7% accuracy on Humanity's Last Exam, representing a 6.9 percentage point lead over the next best system, and 75.7% accuracy on GAIA, a 9.7 percentage point improvement over DeepSeek-V3.1.
Ablation studies confirmed the Iterative Deep-Research Paradigm as the critical performance driver, and the Research-Synthesis Framework improved HLE pass@1 from 28.8% to 35.65% with parallel agents.
solution: Introduction of IterResearch, an iterative deep-research paradigm that reformulates research as a Markov Decision Process, enabling periodic synthesis of findings and reconstruction of focused workspaces.
Development of WebFrontier, a scalable data synthesis engine, which generates large volumes of high-quality, complex, and verifiable training data through a multi-agent iterative workflow.
Implementation of a Research-Synthesis Framework at test-time, utilizing parallel research agents and a synthesis agent to integrate diverse findings for more comprehensive and robust conclusions.
paper_summary.summary: WebResearcher, an AI agent framework from Alibaba Group's Tongyi Lab, introduces an iterative deep-research paradigm and a scalable data synthesis engine to overcome context limitations in long-horizon tasks. It demonstrated state-of-the-art performance across six challenging benchmarks, achieving a 6.9 percentage point improvement on Humanity's Last Exam and a 9.7 percentage point lead on GAIA over previous top systems.

title: WebResearcher: Unleashing unbounded reasoning capability in Long-Horizon Agents

abstract:
Survey paper plays a crucial role in scientific research, especially given
the rapid growth of research publications. Recently, researchers have begun
using LLMs to automate survey generation for better efficiency. However, the
quality gap between LLM-generated surveys and those written by human remains
significant, particularly in terms of outline quality and citation accuracy. To
close these gaps, we introduce SurveyForge, which first generates the outline
by analyzing the logical structure of human-written outlines and referring to
the retrieved domain-related articles. Subsequently, leveraging high-quality
papers retrieved from memory by our scholar navigation agent, SurveyForge can
automatically generate and refine the content of the generated article.
Moreover, to achieve a comprehensive evaluation, we construct SurveyBench,
which includes 100 human-written survey papers for win-rate comparison and
assesses AI-generated survey papers across three dimensions: reference,
outline, and content quality. Experiments demonstrate that SurveyForge can
outperform previous works such as AutoSurvey.

keyInsights: Effective survey generation requires both structural understanding (via outline heuristics) and content synthesis capabilities
Memory mechanisms improve query handling and bridge the gap between outline and content generation
Temporal awareness in citation selection helps balance relevance, impact and recency
originalProblem: Existing automated survey generation methods produce poorly organized outlines and fail to cite key influential literature
The exponential growth of scientific publications makes it difficult for researchers to stay current and write comprehensive surveys
Lack of standardized evaluation metrics for automated survey generation systems
results: Outperforms previous methods in reference quality, outline structure, and content organization
Demonstrates strong alignment with human expert assessments in win-rate comparisons
Achieves superior performance using open-source models (DeepSeek-v3) at lower cost than GPT-4
Successfully reduces barriers to entry for researchers exploring new fields
solution: Two-stage framework combining heuristic outline generation with a memory-driven scholar navigation agent (SANA)
Memory-based mechanisms for query decomposition and information retrieval with temporal-aware reranking
Creation of SurveyBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark with 100 human-written surveys for systematic evaluation
paper_summary.summary: Shanghai AI Lab researchers introduce SURVEYFORGE, a breakthrough framework for automated academic survey writing that combines outline heuristics and memory-driven content generation, demonstrating superior performance in reference quality and outline structure while establishing a new evaluation benchmark (SurveyBench) for assessing automated survey generation systems.

title: SurveyForge: On the Outline Heuristics, Memory-Driven Generation, and  Multi-dimensional Evaluation for Automated Survey Writing

abstract: This paper tackles \textbf{open-ended deep research (OEDR)}, a complex challenge where AI agents must synthesize vast web-scale information into insightful reports. Current approaches are plagued by dual-fold limitations: static research pipelines that decouple planning from evidence acquisition and monolithic generation paradigms that include redundant, irrelevant evidence, suffering from hallucination issues and low citation accuracy. To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{WebWeaver}, a novel dual-agent framework that emulates the human research process. The planner operates in a dynamic cycle, iteratively interleaving evidence acquisition with outline optimization to produce a comprehensive, citation-grounded outline linking to a memory bank of evidence. The writer then executes a hierarchical retrieval and writing process, composing the report section by section. By performing targeted retrieval of only the necessary evidence from the memory bank via citations for each part, it effectively mitigates long-context issues and citation hallucinations. Our framework establishes a new state-of-the-art across major OEDR benchmarks, including DeepResearch Bench, DeepConsult, and DeepResearchGym. These results validate our human-centric, iterative methodology, demonstrating that adaptive planning and focused synthesis are crucial for producing comprehensive, trusted, and well-structured reports.

keyInsights: Emulating human-like iterative research processes, which integrate dynamic planning and adaptive information seeking, is essential for tackling open-ended deep research effectively.
Continuously optimizing the research outline based on newly acquired evidence significantly enhances report comprehensiveness and structural coherence.

Managing context through targeted, memory-grounded retrieval during hierarchical writing is crucial for overcoming "long-context" issues, reducing hallucinations, and ensuring high citation accuracy in complex report generation.
originalProblem: Current AI agents for Open-Ended Deep Research (OEDR) often use static, decoupled planning and information gathering, leading to outdated outlines and limited searches.
Existing methods struggle with monolithic report generation, resulting in "lost in the middle" issues, increased hallucinations, and low citation accuracy when processing vast web-scale information.
The high cost and restrictive access of proprietary LLM-based research agents limit accessibility and inhibit the wider adoption and reproducibility of advanced OEDR capabilities.
results: WebWeaver achieved state-of-the-art performance across Open-Ended Deep Research (OEDR) benchmarks, with its Qwen3-235b configuration scoring 50.62 in overall report quality on DeepResearch Bench.

The framework demonstrated exceptional web retrieval capabilities, achieving a 93.37% citation accuracy and an average of 200.75 effective citations per task on DeepResearch Bench, surpassing proprietary systems like Gemini and OpenAI agents.

A high-quality Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) dataset, WebWeaver-3k, enabled a smaller 30B LLM to dramatically improve its citation accuracy from 25% to 85.90%, democratizing expert-level OEDR capabilities.
solution: A dual-agent framework, WebWeaver, is introduced, comprising a Planner for dynamic evidence acquisition and outline optimization, and a Writer for memory-grounded report synthesis.
The Planner iteratively refines the research outline by interleaving web search actions with outline adjustments, ensuring the plan is continuously updated with new evidence.
The Writer constructs the report section-by-section, retrieving only specific, citation-linked evidence from a Memory Bank to maintain focused context and prevent information overload.
paper_summary.summary: WebWeaver, a dual-agent framework from Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group, structures web-scale evidence for open-ended deep research by employing a dynamic outline optimization planner and a memory-grounded hierarchical writer. The system achieves state-of-the-art performance on OEDR benchmarks, demonstrating improved report quality and a 93.37% citation accuracy, and facilitates fine-tuning smaller LLMs to achieve expert-level research capabilities.

title: WebWeaver: Structuring Web-Scale Evidence with Dynamic Outlines for Open-Ended Deep Research

abstract: Writing effective rebuttals is a high-stakes task that demands more than linguistic fluency, as it requires precise alignment between reviewer intent and manuscript details. Current solutions typically treat this as a direct-to-text generation problem, suffering from hallucination, overlooked critiques, and a lack of verifiable grounding. To address these limitations, we introduce $\textbf{RebuttalAgent}$, the first multi-agents framework that reframes rebuttal generation as an evidence-centric planning task. Our system decomposes complex feedback into atomic concerns and dynamically constructs hybrid contexts by synthesizing compressed summaries with high-fidelity text while integrating an autonomous and on-demand external search module to resolve concerns requiring outside literature. By generating an inspectable response plan before drafting, $\textbf{RebuttalAgent}$ ensures that every argument is explicitly anchored in internal or external evidence. We validate our approach on the proposed $\textbf{RebuttalBench}$ and demonstrate that our pipeline outperforms strong baselines in coverage, faithfulness, and strategic coherence, offering a transparent and controllable assistant for the peer review process. Code will be released.

keyInsights: Reframing rebuttal generation as an "evidence organization" and "decision" problem, rather than solely text generation, is crucial for improving reliability and factual accuracy in high-stakes contexts.

Multi-agent decomposition with specialized roles and explicit intermediate artifacts (e.g., structured concerns, evidence briefs, response plans) effectively mitigates LLM limitations like hallucination and lack of verifiable grounding.

A "verify-then-write" workflow with human-in-the-loop checkpoints empowers authors to validate strategic plans and commitments before drafting, ensuring accountability and preventing misrepresentations.
originalProblem: Scientific authors experience significant cognitive and temporal pressure to craft effective rebuttals under tight deadlines.
Existing large language model (LLM) approaches for rebuttal generation often suffer from hallucination, overlooking critiques, and a lack of verifiable grounding.
Current interactive LLM methods are inefficient and opaque, making it difficult to audit their reasoning or ensure consistent arguments across responses.
results: On the newly introduced REBUTTALBENCH benchmark, *RebuttalAgent* consistently surpassed direct LLM baselines across all evaluation dimensions, showing improvements of up to +0.78 in Coverage and +1.33 in Specificity.
The agentic pipeline provided more pronounced performance gains when utilizing weaker LLM backbones, indicating its effectiveness in enhancing the capabilities of less powerful models.

Ablation studies confirmed that the Evidence Construction module, particularly external literature retrieval, was critical for enabling specific and actionable responses, with input structuring also measurably contributing to semantic alignment.
solution: A multi-agent framework, *RebuttalAgent*, implements a "verify-then-write" workflow, explicitly decoupling complex reasoning from text drafting for enhanced transparency.
The system structures manuscript content and reviewer concerns into atomic units, then constructs evidence bundles using both internal (manuscript) and external (scholarly search) sources.
It generates an explicit response plan, distinguishing between interpretive defense and actionable interventions, with a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before final rebuttal drafting.
paper_summary.summary: Ma et al. developed Paper2Rebuttal, a multi-agent framework to assist authors in crafting scientific rebuttals using a "verify-then-write" workflow. The system enhances transparency and evidence grounding, outperforming direct LLM baselines in relevance and argumentation quality on a new evaluation benchmark.

title: Paper2Rebuttal: A Multi-Agent Framework for Transparent Author Response Assistance

abstract:
The powerful reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in
mathematics and coding, combined with their ability to automate complex tasks
through agentic frameworks, present unprecedented opportunities for
accelerating scientific innovation. In this paper, we introduce AI-Researcher,
a fully autonomous research system that transforms how AI-driven scientific
discovery is conducted and evaluated. Our framework seamlessly orchestrates the
complete research pipeline--from literature review and hypothesis generation to
algorithm implementation and publication-ready manuscript preparation--with
minimal human intervention. To rigorously assess autonomous research
capabilities, we develop Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising
state-of-the-art papers across diverse AI research domains, featuring both
guided innovation and open-ended exploration tasks. Through extensive
experiments, we demonstrate that AI-Researcher achieves remarkable
implementation success rates and produces research papers that approach
human-level quality. This work establishes new foundations for autonomous
scientific innovation that can complement human researchers by systematically
exploring solution spaces beyond cognitive limitations.

keyInsights: Fully autonomous AI systems can orchestrate the entire scientific discovery pipeline, from conceptualization to publication-ready manuscript generation, with high fidelity.

Open-ended autonomous exploration tasks (Level-2) can yield superior scientific quality compared to instruction-guided tasks (Level-1), suggesting that prescriptive instructions might constrain AI creativity.

The choice of the foundational LLM backbone significantly impacts both the implementation success rates and the scientific quality of the generated research, with Claude-series models often outperforming GPT-4o for this task.
originalProblem: Human researchers face limitations (cognitive biases, processing capacity) in navigating the vast and complex landscape of scientific discovery.
Existing AI agent systems, while automating routine tasks, lack the deep intellectual capacity and end-to-end orchestration needed for genuine scientific innovation.
A critical absence of standardized benchmarks hinders systematic evaluation and progress in autonomous scientific research systems.
results: AI-Researcher achieved a 93.8% code implementation completeness rate with Claude-series models on Scientist-Bench, successfully producing executable code for diverse tasks.
AI-generated papers approached human-level quality, with 15.79% to 78.95% (depending on the LLM evaluator) deemed comparable to human-authored top-tier research.

The system demonstrated superior scientific quality in open-ended (Level-2) innovation tasks, achieving higher average ratings and comparable rates (e.g., 40-100% comparable) than instruction-guided (Level-1) tasks.
solution: Introduces AI-Researcher, a multi-agent framework that autonomously orchestrates the complete scientific discovery lifecycle, from literature review and hypothesis generation to algorithm implementation, experimental validation, and manuscript preparation.
Develops a Multi-Stage Refinement Architecture and Expert Validation Framework that mimics human iterative research, enhancing implementation success rates and allowing for test-time scaling.

Presents Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring Level-1 (guided innovation) and Level-2 (autonomous exploration) tasks, along with a two-stage evaluation protocol for technical execution and scientific contribution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The University of Hong Kong developed AI-Researcher, a fully autonomous AI system designed to conduct end-to-end scientific discovery, and introduced Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating autonomous research capabilities. The system achieved high code implementation rates and generated research papers that approached human-level quality, particularly excelling in open-ended scientific innovation tasks.

title: AI-Researcher: Autonomous Scientific Innovation

abstract: This paper introduces Agent-Based Auto Research, a structured multi-agent framework designed to automate, coordinate, and optimize the full lifecycle of scientific research. Leveraging the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and modular agent collaboration, the system spans all major research phases, including literature review, ideation, methodology planning, experimentation, paper writing, peer review response, and dissemination. By addressing issues such as fragmented workflows, uneven methodological expertise, and cognitive overload, the framework offers a systematic and scalable approach to scientific inquiry. Preliminary explorations demonstrate the feasibility and potential of Auto Research as a promising paradigm for self-improving, AI-driven research processes.

keyInsights: The work redefines scientific inquiry as a modular, interpretable, and optimizable process, enabling a systemic integration of human and machine intelligence.
LLM agents can effectively coordinate and automate complex research tasks across the full lifecycle, from generating novel ideas to strategizing research dissemination.
This framework promises to democratize research access, accelerate scientific progress, and enhance reproducibility by mitigating human limitations and cognitive overload.
originalProblem: Traditional scientific research suffers from fragmented workflows, leading to inefficiencies and increased cognitive burden for researchers.
There is an uneven distribution of methodological expertise and limited systematization in problem-solving, impacting research quality and reproducibility.
Researchers experience cognitive overload due to the vast amount of information and diverse skill sets required, hindering deep engagement with core problems.
results: LLM agents successfully clarified research topics, identified gaps in literature, and generated initial LaTeX drafts for literature reviews.
A prototype method planner generated logical research plans, and a heuristic solution designer selected practical techniques for sub-tasks, such as using LSTM for cryptomining detection.

Experimental agents correctly identified appropriate benchmarks for 90% of CV/NLP tasks, aligned baselines with human selections in 78% of cases, and produced executable Python code (72-78% unmodified).
solution: A multi-agent framework is introduced, employing specialized LLM agents for each of the eight phases of the research lifecycle (Literature, Idea, Method, Experiment, Paper, Evaluation, Rebuttal, Promotion).
Agents leverage LLMs for natural language understanding, content generation, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning to decompose problems and make heuristic-driven decisions.
The system dynamically retrieves information from real-time search engines and scholarly databases to ensure up-to-date knowledge and supports iterative refinement and self-optimization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nanyang Technological University and Nankai University propose an "Agent-Based Auto Research" framework, which utilizes a multi-agent system powered by Large Language Models to automate the entire scientific inquiry lifecycle. Preliminary explorations across eight research phases demonstrate the feasibility of agents managing tasks from literature review and ideation to experimental setup, paper drafting, peer review simulation, and research dissemination.

title: A Vision for Auto Research with LLM Agents

abstract: Autonomous systems that generate scientific hypotheses, conduct experiments, and draft manuscripts have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for accelerating discovery. However, existing AI Scientists remain largely domain-agnostic, limiting their applicability to clinical medicine, where research is required to be grounded in medical evidence with specialized data modalities. In this work, we introduce Medical AI Scientist, the first autonomous research framework tailored to clinical autonomous research. It enables clinically grounded ideation by transforming extensively surveyed literature into actionable evidence through clinician-engineer co-reasoning mechanism, which improves the traceability of generated research ideas. It further facilitates evidence-grounded manuscript drafting guided by structured medical compositional conventions and ethical policies. The framework operates under 3 research modes, namely paper-based reproduction, literature-inspired innovation, and task-driven exploration, each corresponding to a distinct level of automated scientific inquiry with progressively increasing autonomy. Comprehensive evaluations by both large language models and human experts demonstrate that the ideas generated by the Medical AI Scientist are of substantially higher quality than those produced by commercial LLMs across 171 cases, 19 clinical tasks, and 6 data modalities. Meanwhile, our system achieves strong alignment between the proposed method and its implementation, while also demonstrating significantly higher success rates in executable experiments. Double-blind evaluations by human experts and the Stanford Agentic Reviewer suggest that the generated manuscripts approach MICCAI-level quality, while consistently surpassing those from ISBI and BIBM. The proposed Medical AI Scientist highlights the potential of leveraging AI for autonomous scientific discovery in healthcare.

keyInsights: Integrating a clinician-engineer co-reasoning mechanism and structured evidence retrieval significantly enhances the clinical grounding, traceability, and ethicality of AI-generated research ideas, mitigating hallucinations.

A multi-stage experimental pipeline within a Dockerized environment, unifying general-purpose tools with domain-specific medical toolboxes, enables robust, traceable, and self-correcting model development despite heterogeneous medical data.

An evidence-grounded manuscript composer guided by medical writing paradigms and explicit ethical policies is crucial for producing high-quality, reproducible, and ethically compliant research papers in the medical domain.
originalProblem: Existing general-purpose AI Scientist systems lack medical-specific priors, leading to clinically irrelevant hypotheses and overlooking specialized data modalities and ethical considerations in medical research.
Medical research faces challenges with heterogeneous data, domain-specific evaluation, and stringent ethical/reporting requirements that current autonomous systems do not adequately address.
The rapid growth of medical datasets, methodologies, and literature creates a significant bottleneck for human-driven research, requiring autonomous solutions to accelerate discovery.
results: The Medical AI Scientist outperformed commercial LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro) in idea generation, scoring higher across six dimensions including novelty (4.07 vs. 3.00 for GPT-5) and maturity (4.61 vs. ≤3.58).

It achieved higher first-run experimental execution success rates across 57 medical AI research instances, reaching 0.91 in reproduction mode, 0.93 in innovation mode, and 0.86 in task-driven mode, significantly surpassing baselines.

Generated manuscripts achieved a mean score of 4.60 ± 0.56 (AI-based evaluation), comparable to MICCAI submissions (4.86 ± 0.47), and one system-generated paper was accepted by ICAIS 2025 (36.8% acceptance rate).
solution: The "Medical AI Scientist" framework integrates a multi-agent system comprising an Idea Proposer, Experimental Executor, and Manuscript Composer to manage the entire medical AI research lifecycle.

It operates in three modes: Paper-based Reproduction for faithful implementation, Literature-inspired Innovation for gap identification and hypothesis generation (with clinician-engineer co-reasoning), and Task-driven Exploration for problem-driven discovery.

A new benchmark, Med-AI Bench, was developed with 171 evaluation cases across 19 medical research tasks and 6 data modalities to enable rigorous, domain-spanning assessment of the framework's performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduce the "Medical AI Scientist," an autonomous framework for end-to-end medical AI research, designed to generate clinically relevant hypotheses, execute experiments, and draft manuscripts while adhering to medical ethical guidelines. The system demonstrates superior performance compared to commercial large language models in idea quality, achieves high success rates in experimental execution across diverse medical tasks, and produces publication-quality manuscripts, with one paper accepted to an international conference.

title: Towards a Medical AI Scientist

abstract: High-quality scientific illustrations are crucial for effectively communicating complex scientific and technical concepts, yet their manual creation remains a well-recognized bottleneck in both academia and industry. We present FigureBench, the first large-scale benchmark for generating scientific illustrations from long-form scientific texts. It contains 3,300 high-quality scientific text-figure pairs, covering diverse text-to-illustration tasks from scientific papers, surveys, blogs, and textbooks. Moreover, we propose AutoFigure, the first agentic framework that automatically generates high-quality scientific illustrations based on long-form scientific text. Specifically, before rendering the final result, AutoFigure engages in extensive thinking, recombination, and validation to produce a layout that is both structurally sound and aesthetically refined, outputting a scientific illustration that achieves both structural completeness and aesthetic appeal. Leveraging the high-quality data from FigureBench, we conduct extensive experiments to test the performance of AutoFigure against various baseline methods. The results demonstrate that AutoFigure consistently surpasses all baseline methods, producing publication-ready scientific illustrations. The code, dataset and huggingface space are released in this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoupling structural planning from aesthetic rendering within a "Reasoned Rendering" paradigm is crucial for achieving both semantic fidelity and visual appeal in complex scientific illustrations.
An iterative critique-and-refine loop, simulating human design processes, effectively enhances the logical consistency and structural coherence of generated layouts.
Dedicated post-processing for text, such as an "Erase-and-Correct" strategy, is essential to overcome common text rendering inaccuracies in generative models and ensure publication-ready quality.
originalProblem: Manually creating high-quality scientific illustrations is a time-consuming and labor-intensive process, demanding deep logical understanding, design skills, and domain expertise.
Existing automated methods struggle with long scientific texts, often failing to maintain structural fidelity, content accuracy, and aesthetic appeal simultaneously.
The "AI Scientists" paradigm is limited by the inability of AI systems to autonomously generate and visually articulate their complex discoveries.
results: AUTOFIGURE consistently achieved the highest "Overall" scores in VLM-as-a-judge referenced evaluations across all four document categories, outperforming baselines.
In human expert evaluations, 66.7% of domain experts expressed willingness to use AUTOFIGURE's generated figures in their own camera-ready papers, with an 83.3% win rate against other AI models.

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of the iterative critique-and-refine loop (improving overall scores from 6.28 to 7.14) and the aesthetic rendering stage (boosting overall scores from 6.38 to 7.48).
solution: Introduced FigureBench, the first large-scale benchmark for "Long-context Scientific Illustration Design," comprising 3,300 high-quality scientific text-figure pairs from diverse document types.
Developed AUTOFIGURE, an agentic framework employing a "Reasoned Rendering" paradigm that decouples structural layout generation from aesthetic rendering.
The framework incorporates a critique-and-refine loop for optimizing structural fidelity and an "Erase-and-Correct" post-processing step to ensure textual accuracy in the final illustration.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Westlake University developed AUTOFIGURE, an agentic framework that autonomously generates publication-ready scientific illustrations directly from extensive scientific texts. The system achieved a 66.7% publication intent from human experts and consistently outperformed other AI models in automated evaluations by balancing structural fidelity, content accuracy, and aesthetic appeal through its "Reasoned Rendering" approach.

title: AutoFigure: Generating and Refining Publication-Ready Scientific Illustrations

abstract: The ability to research and synthesize knowledge is central to human expertise and progress. A new class of AI systems--designed for generative research synthesis--aims to automate this process by retrieving information from the live web and producing long-form, cited reports. Yet, evaluating such systems remains an open challenge: existing question-answering benchmarks focus on short, factual answers, while expert-curated datasets risk staleness and data contamination. Neither captures the complexity and evolving nature of real research synthesis tasks. We introduce DeepScholar-bench, a live benchmark and automated evaluation framework for generative research synthesis. DeepScholar-bench draws queries and human-written exemplars from recent, high-quality ArXiv papers and evaluates a real synthesis task: generating a related work section by retrieving, synthesizing, and citing prior work. Our automated framework holistically measures performance across three key dimensions--knowledge synthesis, retrieval quality, and verifiability. To further future work, we also contribute DeepScholar-ref, a simple, open-source reference pipeline, which is implemented on the LOTUS framework and provides a strong baseline. Using DeepScholar-bench, we systematically evaluate prior open-source systems, search agents with strong models, OpenAI's DeepResearch, and DeepScholar-ref. We find DeepScholar-bench is far from saturated: no system surpasses a geometric mean of $31\%$ across all metrics. These results highlight both the difficulty and importance of DeepScholar-bench as a foundation for advancing AI systems capable of generative research synthesis. We make our benchmark code and data available at this https URL.
title: DeepScholar-Bench: A Live Benchmark and Automated Evaluation for Generative Research Synthesis

abstract: The automation of scientific discovery represents a critical milestone in Artificial Intelligence (AI) research. However, existing agentic systems for science suffer from two fundamental limitations: rigid, pre-programmed workflows that cannot adapt to intermediate findings, and inadequate context management that hinders long-horizon research. We present \texttt{freephdlabor}, an open-source multiagent framework featuring \textit{fully dynamic workflows} determined by real-time agent reasoning and a \coloremph{\textit{modular architecture}} enabling seamless customization -- users can modify, add, or remove agents to address domain-specific requirements. The framework provides comprehensive infrastructure including \textit{automatic context compaction}, \textit{workspace-based communication} to prevent information degradation, \textit{memory persistence} across sessions, and \textit{non-blocking human intervention} mechanisms. These features collectively transform automated research from isolated, single-run attempts into \textit{continual research programs} that build systematically on prior explorations and incorporate human feedback. By providing both the architectural principles and practical implementation for building customizable co-scientist systems, this work aims to facilitate broader adoption of automated research across scientific domains, enabling practitioners to deploy interactive multiagent systems that autonomously conduct end-to-end research -- from ideation through experimentation to publication-ready manuscripts.

keyInsights: Dynamic workflows emerging from real-time agent reasoning enable true adaptability and responsiveness in scientific discovery, unlike rigid pre-programmed pipelines.
Robust, lossless communication via a shared file-based workspace is essential to prevent information degradation in complex, long-horizon multi-agent interactions.
A modular, open-source design significantly lowers barriers to entry, allowing scientists to customize AI agents for specific domain needs and fostering wider adoption.
originalProblem: Existing AI science automation systems rely on rigid, pre-programmed workflows, hindering adaptability to evolving research contexts or unexpected findings.
Large Language Models suffer from limited context windows, leading to information loss and distortion over iterative interactions in long-horizon scientific tasks.
Inter-agent communication in multi-agent systems often results in 'information degradation' or the 'game of telephone effect,' impeding reliable coordination.
results: Successfully completed an end-to-end research project on 'HMM-based Training Phase Detection,' from ideation to a publication-ready manuscript.
Demonstrated dynamic error recovery, where the ManagerAgent autonomously identified and resolved a workspace configuration issue without human intervention.

Achieved quality-driven iteration, utilizing a ReviewerAgent to assess paper drafts and orchestrating comprehensive revisions, ultimately improving the paper's quality to an 'Accept with Minor Revisions' equivalent.
solution: Employs a star-shaped multi-agent architecture with a central ManagerAgent that dynamically orchestrates tasks using the ReAct framework, adapting workflows in real-time.
Utilizes a shared, file-based workspace for inter-agent communication, ensuring lossless data exchange and acting as persistent external memory.
Incorporates features for continual research, including automatic context compaction, memory persistence, and non-blocking human intervention for long-term collaboration.
paper_summary.summary: freephdlabor introduces an open-source, multi-agent framework that orchestrates dynamic scientific research workflows, enabling AI agents to conduct continuous, interactive, and customizable end-to-end discovery. This system demonstrates its capability by autonomously completing a research project, including error recovery and quality-driven revisions, from ideation to a publication-ready manuscript.

title: Build Your Personalized Research Group: A Multiagent Framework for Continual and Interactive Science Automation

abstract:
Efficient experiment reproduction is critical to accelerating progress in
artificial intelligence. However, the inherent complexity of method design and
training procedures presents substantial challenges for automation. Notably,
reproducing experiments often requires implicit domain-specific knowledge not
explicitly documented in the original papers. To address this, we introduce the
paper lineage algorithm, which identifies and extracts implicit knowledge from
the relevant references cited by the target paper. Building on this idea, we
propose AutoReproduce, a multi-agent framework capable of automatically
reproducing experiments described in research papers in an end-to-end manner.
AutoReproduce enhances code executability by generating unit tests alongside
the reproduction process. To evaluate the reproduction capability, we construct
ReproduceBench, a benchmark annotated with verified implementations, and
introduce novel evaluation metrics to assess both the reproduction and
execution fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that AutoReproduce
outperforms the existing strong agent baselines on all five evaluation metrics
by a peak margin of over $70\%$. In particular, compared to the official
implementations, AutoReproduce achieves an average performance gap of $22.1\%$
on $89.74\%$ of the executable experiment runs. The code will be available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Incorporating knowledge from cited papers and their codebases through the 'paper lineage' algorithm is essential for inferring implicit implementation details and improving reproduction accuracy.

Iterative debugging and refinement processes, including unit testing with batch sampling and detailed data attribute understanding, are crucial for achieving high code executability and minimizing performance discrepancies.

A collaborative multi-agent LLM framework can successfully manage complex, end-to-end scientific tasks, moving beyond simple code generation to encompass comprehension, reasoning, and verification for scientific automation.
originalProblem: Efficient AI experiment reproduction is hampered by the inherent complexity of method design and training procedures, consuming significant time and specialized expertise.
Research papers often rely on 'implicit domain-specific knowledge' not explicitly documented, making accurate manual reproduction exceptionally difficult.

Existing LLM-driven automation tools primarily address discrete experimental stages or offer partial assistance, lacking comprehensive end-to-end frameworks for automatic reproduction with guaranteed executability and performance fidelity.
results: AUTOREPRODUCE achieved an 89.74% execution rate and a 30.13% performance gap on the REPRODUCEBENCH benchmark, significantly outperforming baselines like ChatDev (2.56% Exec Rate) and PaperCoder (34.46% Performance Gap).
Ablation studies confirm the paper lineage algorithm and the debugging and refinement processes are key contributors to improvements in both code alignment and performance gap metrics.

Human evaluations consistently rated AUTOREPRODUCE's generated code as higher quality, scoring substantially better across method (7.23 vs. 4.08), parameter (3.69 vs. 2.85), and experiment (3.83 vs. 1.92) aspects compared to strong baselines.
solution: A multi-agent framework called AUTOREPRODUCE employs a collaborative setup between a 'research agent' for text-centric tasks and a 'code agent' for code-oriented tasks, structured into literature review, paper lineage, and code development phases.
A novel 'paper lineage' algorithm addresses implicit knowledge by identifying relevant cited papers and their code repositories, extracting common implementation practices and contextual references.

The code development phase involves iterative refinement, data acquisition with batch sampling for attribute understanding, and method replication with unit testing and debugging to ensure generated code is executable and functionally aligned with the paper.
paper_summary.summary: AUTOREPRODUCE is an automated, end-to-end framework for reproducing AI experiments described in research papers, integrating a novel paper lineage algorithm and iterative debugging to address implicit knowledge and ensure high execution fidelity. It demonstrates superior performance over existing baselines in generating executable code that closely matches the performance of official implementations.

title: AutoReproduce: Automatic AI Experiment Reproduction with Paper Lineage

abstract: Recent advancements in AI agents have demonstrated their growing potential to drive and support scientific discovery. In this work, we introduce MLR-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating AI agents on open-ended machine learning research. MLR-Bench includes three key components: (1) 201 research tasks sourced from NeurIPS, ICLR, and ICML workshops covering diverse ML topics; (2) MLR-Judge, an automated evaluation framework combining LLM-based reviewers with carefully designed review rubrics to assess research quality; and (3) MLR-Agent, a modular agent scaffold capable of completing research tasks through four stages: idea generation, proposal formulation, experimentation, and paper writing. Our framework supports both stepwise assessment across these distinct research stages, and end-to-end evaluation of the final research paper. We then use MLR-Bench to evaluate six frontier LLMs and an advanced coding agent, finding that while LLMs are effective at generating coherent ideas and well-structured papers, current coding agents frequently (e.g., in 80% of the cases) produce fabricated or invalidated experimental results--posing a major barrier to scientific reliability. We validate MLR-Judge through human evaluation, showing high agreement with expert reviewers, supporting its potential as a scalable tool for research evaluation. We open-source MLR-Bench to help the community benchmark, diagnose, and improve AI research agents toward trustworthy and transparent scientific discovery.

organization_info.name: Singapore University of Technology and Design

keyInsights: Current frontier AI agents consistently struggle with scientific soundness, often resorting to 'experiment results hallucination' by fabricating data when execution failures occur, prioritizing completeness over correctness.
The LLM-based MLR-Judge demonstrated strong agreement with human expert reviewers, making it a reliable and scalable tool for automated evaluation of AI-generated research.
AI agents exhibit a notable performance gap in generating novel and feasible research ideas and proposals, despite strong capabilities in logical coherence and significance.
originalProblem: A lack of comprehensive and systematic evaluation frameworks for AI agents operating in open-ended scientific research.
Existing benchmarks for AI agents target narrower, task-specific aspects, failing to assess an agent's ability to navigate the full, iterative process of scientific inquiry.
Difficulty in reliably measuring progress and identifying specific weaknesses, such as hallucination or lack of novelty, in AI-driven research outputs.
results: AI agents achieved strong performance in consistency and significance for idea and proposal generation but scored below 7.5 on novelty, soundness, and feasibility.
Coding agents (Claude Code, Codex) struggled significantly in the experimentation stage, with overall ratings below a 6.0 acceptance threshold and notably low scores for soundness and insightfulness.
MLR-Judge's evaluations showed no statistically significant differences (p-values > 0.05) when compared to human expert evaluations across five key criteria, validating its reliability.
End-to-end evaluations revealed that all tested AI agents, including AI Scientist V2, failed to meet the 6.0 acceptance threshold, with 'soundness' consistently being their lowest-scoring dimension.
solution: Introduced MLR-Bench, a benchmark featuring 201 real-world, open-ended machine learning research tasks sourced from top-tier conference workshops.
Developed MLR-Judge, an LLM-based automated evaluation system utilizing structured rubrics to assess research quality, with validated alignment to human expert reviews.
Provided MLR-Agent, a modular agent scaffold supporting both stepwise execution (idea generation, proposal, experimentation, paper writing) and end-to-end research task completion.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the National University of Singapore and collaborators developed MLR-Bench, a new benchmark to evaluate AI agents on open-ended machine learning research, comprising 201 real-world tasks, an LLM-based judge, and an agent scaffold. Their findings indicate current AI agents struggle with scientific soundness and often hallucinate experimental results, while the LLM judge's evaluations align closely with human experts.

title: MLR-Bench: Evaluating AI Agents on Open-Ended Machine Learning Research

abstract: As an embodiment of intelligence evolution toward interconnected architectures, Deep Research Agents (DRAs) systematically exhibit the capabilities in task decomposition, cross-source retrieval, multi-stage reasoning, information integration, and structured output, which markedly enhance performance on complex and open-ended tasks. However, existing benchmarks remain deficient in evaluation dimensions, response format, and scoring mechanisms, limiting their effectiveness in assessing such agents. This paper introduces Dr. Bench, a multidimensional evaluation framework tailored to DRAs and long-form report-style responses. The benchmark comprises 214 expert-curated challenging tasks across 10 broad domains, each accompanied by manually constructed reference bundles to support composite evaluation. This framework incorporates metrics for semantic quality, topical focus, and retrieval trustworthiness, enabling a comprehensive evaluation of long reports generated by DRAs. Extensive experimentation confirms the superior performance of mainstream DRAs over web-search-tool-augmented reasoning models, yet reveals considerable scope for further improvement. This study provides a robust foundation for capability assessment, architectural refinement, and paradigm advancement of DRAs.

keyInsights: Evaluating advanced AI agents in complex, long-form report generation tasks necessitates a shift from short-text outputs and isolated skill assessments to capturing integrated capabilities through multidimensional evaluation.

A comprehensive framework that considers semantic quality, topical focus, and retrieval trustworthiness provides a more accurate, stable, and holistic assessment of Deep Research Agent performance compared to traditional surface-level metrics.

The use of expert-curated tasks and detailed reference bundles is crucial for establishing rigorous, transparent, and verifiable evaluation standards that genuinely reflect the expectations for AI-generated research outputs.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for advanced AI agents primarily focus on discrete, short-text outputs and isolated competencies, failing to adequately evaluate the complex, long-form report generation capabilities of Deep Research Agents (DRAs).

Current evaluation metrics, such as string matching or LLM-as-judger similarity, are superficial, subjective, and unstable, providing insufficient assessment of semantic depth, structural fidelity, citation authority, or source validity for complex reports.

Deep Research Agents (DRAs), which are designed for multi-source retrieval, multi-stage reasoning, and structured information integration, lack a comprehensive and rigorous evaluation framework tailored to their integrated intelligence.
results: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) consistently demonstrated superior performance in overall task execution and report generation quality compared to reasoning models augmented with web-search tools.

Top-performing DRAs like Qwen-deep-research achieved `IntegratedScore`s of 34.6480, and Sonar-deep-research scored 33.4668, while advanced agent models like Kimi-K2-0905-preview attained the highest `Quality` score (0.6707).

Experiments revealed trade-offs between comprehensive processing and computational cost (high token usage for some DRAs), and showed varying performance across domains, with leading models exhibiting particular strengths in areas like Sports & Competitions and Health & Medicine.
solution: Introduction of DR. BENCH, a benchmark featuring 214 challenging, expert-curated, report-oriented tasks across 10 diverse domains, each supported by comprehensive reference bundles including query-specific rubrics, general-report rubrics, trustworthy sources, and keywords.

Development of a systematic and multidimensional evaluation framework for long-form outputs, assessing semantic quality, topical focus (semantic drift), and retrieval trustworthiness (source credibility), combined into a single `IntegratedScore`.

Implementation of a multi-stage, human-in-the-loop review pipeline during dataset construction, leveraging LLM auditing and multiple expert reviews to ensure high difficulty, quality, and semantic validity of the benchmark data.
paper_summary.summary: DR. BENCH, a new benchmark and multidimensional evaluation framework, assesses Deep Research Agents (DRAs) producing long-form reports using 214 expert-curated tasks across 10 domains. This framework, which measures semantic quality, topical focus, and retrieval trustworthiness, revealed DRAs generally outperform tool-augmented LLMs, with leading models achieving integrated scores above 33 and demonstrated varying strengths in different domains.

title: Dr. Bench: A Multidimensional Evaluation for Deep Research Agents, from Answers to Reports

abstract: DeepResearch agents represent a transformative AI paradigm, conducting expert-level research through sophisticated reasoning and multi-tool integration. However, evaluating these systems remains critically challenging due to open-ended research scenarios and existing benchmarks that focus on isolated capabilities rather than holistic performance. Unlike traditional LLM tasks, DeepResearch systems must synthesize diverse sources, generate insights, and present coherent findings, which are capabilities that resist simple verification. To address this gap, we introduce DeepResearch-ReportEval, a comprehensive framework designed to assess DeepResearch systems through their most representative outputs: research reports. Our approach systematically measures three dimensions: quality, redundancy, and factuality, using an innovative LLM-as-a-Judge methodology achieving strong expert concordance. We contribute a standardized benchmark of 100 curated queries spanning 12 real-world categories, enabling systematic capability comparison. Our evaluation of four leading commercial systems reveals distinct design philosophies and performance trade-offs, establishing foundational insights as DeepResearch evolves from information assistants toward intelligent research partners. Source code and data are available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: The LLM-as-a-Judge approach demonstrates strong alignment with human expert evaluations, providing a reliable and scalable method for assessing complex AI agent outputs.
There exists an "optimal" report length (around 5000-7000 tokens), where systems balance comprehensive coverage with digestible presentation more effectively than overly brief or verbose outputs.
Factuality, demonstrated through robust evidence grounding and accurate source attribution, is a crucial differentiator among leading DeepResearch systems and foundational for user trust.
originalProblem: Existing LLM evaluation benchmarks are inadequate for assessing the holistic, end-to-end performance of complex DeepResearch agents.

There is a lack of a comprehensive, widely accepted framework to evaluate research-oriented AI agents through their primary outputs: research reports, which demand rigorous methodology and evidence synthesis.
Evaluating capabilities like intent clarification, multi-tool integration, systematic evidence collection, validation, and comprehensive synthesis into coherent reports remains challenging.
results: The LLM-as-a-Judge achieved consistently low MAD values (Quality: 0.72, Redundancy: 0.31, Factuality: 0.29) and a 61.11% exact match accuracy in ranking compared to human experts.
Perplexity produced the most concise reports (1245.2 tokens) with the lowest redundancy (3.71), but scored lowest in comprehensiveness (3.16) and insightfulness (2.96).

Qwen achieved the highest overall quality (3.54), comprehensiveness (3.80), and insightfulness (3.38), while both Qwen and OpenAI demonstrated strong factuality with high average support scores and strong support rates.
solution: Introduces DEEPRESEARCH-REPORTEVAL, an evaluation framework designed to assess DeepResearch systems through a multi-dimensional analysis of their research reports.
The framework systematically measures overall quality (comprehensiveness, coherence, clarity, insightfulness), redundancy, and factuality, using specific sub-criteria and scoring mechanisms.

Employs an "LLM-as-a-Judge" methodology, rigorously aligned with human expert judgments and validated using Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) and ranking comparisons, to ensure reliability and scalability.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Hong Kong, Alibaba Group, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, and City University of Hong Kong developed DEEPRESEARCH-REPORTEVAL, a framework to holistically evaluate DeepResearch agents through their research reports. This framework utilizes a multi-dimensional LLM-as-a-Judge approach, validated against human experts, to assess report quality, redundancy, and factuality, revealing performance trade-offs among commercial systems.

title: Understanding DeepResearch via Reports

abstract: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) can autonomously conduct complex investigations and generate comprehensive reports, demonstrating strong real-world potential. However, existing evaluations mostly rely on close-ended benchmarks, while open-ended deep research benchmarks remain scarce and typically neglect personalized scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce Personalized Deep Research Bench (PDR-Bench), the first benchmark for evaluating personalization in DRAs. It pairs 50 diverse research tasks across 10 domains with 25 authentic user profiles that combine structured persona attributes with dynamic real-world contexts, yielding 250 realistic user-task queries. To assess system performance, we propose the PQR Evaluation Framework, which jointly measures Personalization Alignment, Content Quality, and Factual Reliability. Our experiments on a range of systems highlight current capabilities and limitations in handling personalized deep research. This work establishes a rigorous foundation for developing and evaluating the next generation of truly personalized AI research assistants.
title: Towards Personalized Deep Research: Benchmarks and Evaluations

abstract: Academic survey writing, which distills vast literature into a coherent and insightful narrative, remains a labor-intensive and intellectually demanding task. While recent approaches, such as general DeepResearch agents and survey-specialized methods, can generate surveys automatically (a.k.a. LLM4Survey), their outputs often fall short of human standards and there lacks a rigorous, reader-aligned benchmark for thoroughly revealing their deficiencies. To fill the gap, we propose a fine-grained, quiz-driven evaluation framework SurveyBench, featuring (1) typical survey topics source from recent 11,343 arXiv papers and corresponding 4,947 high-quality surveys; (2) a multifaceted metric hierarchy that assesses the outline quality (e.g., coverage breadth, logical coherence), content quality (e.g., synthesis granularity, clarity of insights), and non-textual richness; and (3) a dual-mode evaluation protocol that includes content-based and quiz-based answerability tests, explicitly aligned with readers' informational needs. Results show SurveyBench effectively challenges existing LLM4Survey approaches (e.g., on average 21% lower than human in content-based evaluation).
title: SurveyBench: Can LLM(-Agents) Write Academic Surveys that Align with Reader Needs?

abstract: This paper introduces the first systematic evaluation framework for quantifying the quality and risks of papers written by modern coding agents. While AI-driven paper writing has become a growing concern, rigorous evaluation of the quality and potential risks of AI-written papers remains limited, and a unified understanding of their reliability is still lacking. We introduce Paper Reconstruction Evaluation (PaperRecon), an evaluation framework in which an overview (this http URL) is created from an existing paper, after which an agent generates a full paper based on the overview and minimal additional resources, and the result is subsequently compared against the original paper. PaperRecon disentangles the evaluation of the AI-written papers into two orthogonal dimensions, Presentation and Hallucination, where Presentation is evaluated using a rubric and Hallucination is assessed via agentic evaluation grounded in the original paper source. For evaluation, we introduce PaperWrite-Bench, a benchmark of 51 papers from top-tier venues across diverse domains published after 2025. Our experiments reveal a clear trade-off: while both ClaudeCode and Codex improve with model advances, ClaudeCode achieves higher presentation quality at the cost of more than 10 hallucinations per paper on average, whereas Codex produces fewer hallucinations but lower presentation quality. This work takes a first step toward establishing evaluation frameworks for AI-driven paper writing and improving the understanding of its risks within the research community.

keyInsights: A fundamental trade-off exists between an AI agent's ability to achieve high presentation quality and its tendency to generate factual errors (hallucinations).
Providing more detailed input context, such as a longer `research_overview.md`, consistently improves both the presentation quality and reduces hallucinations in AI-generated papers.

AI writing agent performance varies across scientific domains, with papers from NLP conferences generally achieving higher average rubric scores and fewer hallucinations compared to those from Machine Learning, Computer Vision, and Multimedia.
originalProblem: Existing evaluation methods for AI-written papers are inadequate, often failing to detect severe factual errors or fabrications and sometimes favoring papers with such issues.
A unified framework was lacking to systematically assess both the presentation quality and factual correctness (hallucination) of AI-generated scientific papers.

The complexity of scientific writing, requiring nuanced skills like problem justification and experimental design, poses a significant challenge for AI systems that current evaluations do not sufficiently capture.
results: Claude Code agents achieved higher average presentation rubric scores (e.g., 3.86 with Sonnet 4.6) compared to Codex agents (e.g., 3.59 with GPT-5.4).
Codex agents produced substantially fewer major hallucinations (approximately 3.0 per paper with GPT-5.4) than Claude Code agents (approximately 10.4 per paper with Sonnet 4.6).

Model advancements from earlier to later versions (e.g., GPT-5 to GPT-5.4, Sonnet 4 to Sonnet 4.6) consistently showed increases in rubric scores and decreases in hallucination counts, demonstrating the framework's sensitivity to performance gains.
solution: Developed Paper Reconstruction Evaluation (PaperRecon), a framework that tasks AI agents with reconstructing full scientific papers from minimal structured inputs.

Introduced a comprehensive evaluation protocol assessing generated papers against human-written originals across three dimensions: presentation quality (using LLM-generated rubrics), hallucination (claim-level factual errors), and citation accuracy.
Created PaperWrite-Bench, a benchmark of 51 recent scientific papers from diverse top-tier conferences, to facilitate systematic and realistic evaluation of AI agents.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The University of Tokyo introduced Paper Reconstruction Evaluation (PaperRecon), a framework and benchmark (PaperWrite-Bench) designed to systematically evaluate AI-generated scientific papers for presentation quality and factual accuracy. Their findings revealed a trade-off where agents achieving higher presentation quality often produced more factual errors, while more advanced models showed improvements in both areas.

title: Paper Reconstruction Evaluation: Evaluating Presentation and Hallucination in AI-written Papers

abstract: This paper introduces Leaderboard Auto Generation (LAG), a novel and well-organized framework for automatic generation of leaderboards on a given research topic in rapidly evolving fields like Artificial Intelligence (AI). Faced with a large number of AI papers updated daily, it becomes difficult for researchers to track every paper's proposed methods, experimental results, and settings, prompting the need for efficient automatic leaderboard construction. While large language models (LLMs) offer promise in automating this process, challenges such as multi-document summarization, leaderboard generation, and experiment fair comparison still remain under exploration. LAG solves these challenges through a systematic approach that involves the paper collection, experiment results extraction and integration, leaderboard generation, and quality evaluation. Our contributions include a comprehensive solution to the leaderboard construction problem, a reliable evaluation method, and experimental results showing the high quality of leaderboards.
title: League: Leaderboard Generation on Demand

abstract: The exponential growth of scientific literature poses unprecedented challenges for researchers attempting to synthesize knowledge across rapidly evolving fields. We present \textbf{Agentic AutoSurvey}, a multi-agent framework for automated survey generation that addresses fundamental limitations in existing approaches. Our system employs four specialized agents (Paper Search Specialist, Topic Mining \& Clustering, Academic Survey Writer, and Quality Evaluator) working in concert to generate comprehensive literature surveys with superior synthesis quality. Through experiments on six representative LLM research topics from COLM 2024 categories, we demonstrate that our multi-agent approach achieves significant improvements over existing baselines, scoring 8.18/10 compared to AutoSurvey's 4.77/10. The multi-agent architecture processes 75--443 papers per topic (847 total across six topics) while targeting high citation coverage (often $\geq$80\% on 75--100-paper sets; lower on very large sets such as RLHF) through specialized agent orchestration. Our 12-dimension evaluation captures organization, synthesis integration, and critical analysis beyond basic metrics. These findings demonstrate that multi-agent architectures represent a meaningful advancement for automated literature survey generation in rapidly evolving scientific domains.

keyInsights: Specialized multi-agent orchestration significantly improves the quality of automated academic surveys across all dimensions, including core quality, writing quality, and content depth.
Automated systems can achieve sophisticated thematic integration, identify emergent patterns, and pinpoint research gaps, moving beyond simple paper enumeration.
The comprehensive 12-dimensional evaluation framework provides a robust and nuanced standard for assessing the quality of AI-generated academic content, pushing towards more rigorous assessment.
originalProblem: The exponential growth of scientific literature, especially in rapidly evolving fields like LLMs, creates overwhelming challenges for researchers to manually synthesize knowledge.
Existing automated survey generation tools produce inadequate synthesis quality, offer limited citation coverage (typically 60-70%), and rely on simplistic evaluation frameworks.
Researchers struggle to keep pace with new developments, identify emerging patterns, and conduct comprehensive literature reviews due to the sheer volume of new publications.
results: Agentic AutoSurvey achieved an average quality score of 8.18/10, representing a 71% improvement over the AutoSurvey baseline's score of 4.77/10.
The system demonstrated high efficiency, processing collections of 75 to 443 papers per topic (847 total) in an impressive 15-20 minutes.

It showed a 99% improvement in 'Core Quality' (citation coverage, accuracy, synthesis) and a 68% improvement in 'Writing Quality' compared to the baseline, effectively organizing papers into meaningful thematic clusters.
